rationale: RocksDB is an LSM-tree storage engine where user-facing read/write operations execute on application threads (e.g., db_bench workers) while internal maintenance — compaction, memtable flush, and obsolete file purging — runs on dedicated background thread pools named 'rocksdb:bottom', 'rocksdb:low', and 'rocksdb:high'. Under CPU oversubscription, these background threads compete with foreground threads for CPU time, causing tail latency spikes on reads and writes. By deprioritizing all 'rocksdb:*' threads via a custom BPF scheduler, foreground threads get preferential CPU access, dramatically reducing P99/P99.9 latency while background maintenance continues at reduced priority with minimal throughput impact.

threads.purpose: Lowest-priority compaction pool. Handles large, long-running LSM-tree compactions that are explicitly scheduled at BOTTOM priority to minimize interference with other operations.

Regular compaction pool and flush overflow. Handles standard LSM-tree compactions and memtable flushes when the HIGH priority pool is saturated. This is the primary compaction pool controlled by max_background_compactions.

Memtable flush and file purge pool. Flushes in-memory write buffers (memtables) to SST files on disk and purges obsolete files. While flushes are more time-sensitive than compaction (write stalls can occur if flushes fall behind), they are not on the direct user read/write request path.
User-priority thread pool. Rarely used in standard configurations; available for application-specific background scheduling via the Env API.
